issues.comment: In `deleteScheduledEmailReminder`, when `immediateDelete=true` and `referenceId` is non-null, the function cancels the SendGrid scheduled send but then returns without deleting the `WorkflowReminder` DB record or setting `cancelled: true`. This orphans DB records permanently. The cron job won't pick them up because `cancelled` is never set to `true`, and there is no cascade to clean them up. Contrast with the non-`immediateDelete` path which correctly marks `cancelled: true`, and with `deleteScheduledSMSReminder` which always deletes the DB record.

Both `deleteScheduledEmailReminder` and `deleteScheduledSMSReminder` are `async` functions, but they are called without `await` inside a synchronous `forEach` callback. Their returned promises are discarded. The `try/catch` will never catch errors from these async operations—it can only catch synchronous errors. The developer clearly intended error handling here, but it is completely non-functional for the async code paths (SendGrid API calls, Twilio cancellations, Prisma DB operations).

If the SendGrid API call fails for any single reminder, the entire `for` loop exits to the `catch` block. This means: (1) All subsequent reminders in the batch are not processed (their SendGrid sends are not cancelled and could still fire). (2) The `Promise.all(workflowRemindersToDelete)` is never reached, so even the DB records for successfully cancelled reminders are not deleted (Prisma promises are lazy and won't execute without being awaited). The error handling should be per-reminder (e.g., `Promise.allSettled` or a try/catch inside the loop).

The new code calls the async delete functions (`deleteScheduledEmailReminder`, `deleteScheduledSMSReminder`) without awaiting them and removes `remindersToDelete` from the awaited promises array. This is a regression from the old code which collected DB deletion promises and explicitly awaited them. The handler can return a response to the client before the SMS is actually cancelled in Twilio and before the DB record is deleted. If the SMS delete fails silently (caught internally), the record persists with no retry mechanism (unlike email which has the cron fallback).
